critic_raw:
The feature engineering section explicitly states that all transformers are fit on "combined training and validation data (months 1–12)" rather than training data alone. This means validation-set statistics — including seasonal distributions, geographic patterns, and traffic variability from months 10–12 — directly inform the feature scaling applied to the RL agent during training. The justification given is "to stabilize parameter estimates given seasonal and geographic variability," but using validation data in preprocessing is a form of hyperparameter leakage: the preprocessing pipeline effectively adapts to the validation distribution, which the model selection step also uses. This creates a circular dependency where both preprocessing and model selection are tuned to the same validation data.

The RL reward function penalizes delivery time and customer satisfaction drops jointly via a fixed penalty coefficient of 5 per satisfaction unit below 4.5. Customer satisfaction ratings are a lagged, subjective outcome — they are not available at decision time and cannot be incorporated into the reward at routing decision time. If the reward uses realized satisfaction ratings during training, the agent is learning from information it cannot access at deployment, making the learned policy non-deployable without modification. The description does not clarify whether satisfaction is used as a retrospective training signal or a real-time reward component.

The DQN is trained for only 50–100 episodes on a delivery routing problem where the state space includes continuous traffic, weather, and geographic variables across an urban area. This is an extremely small number of training episodes for deep RL on a high-dimensional continuous-state problem; standard DQN implementations for logistics routing require orders of magnitude more training steps. Combined with a replay buffer of only 10,000 transitions, the agent is unlikely to have learned a stable policy, making any comparison against a heuristic baseline inconclusive.
defender_raw:
This Deep Q-Learning vs. heuristic routing experiment has a notably honest design element that distinguishes it from many RL comparison papers: the incumbent production heuristic is used as the baseline without hyperparameter tuning, because the heuristic has no learnable parameters. This is operationally honest — the DQN agent is being asked to beat the actual deployed system, not a re-tuned version of it, which is the practical question that stakeholders care about. The design does not weaken the baseline to make the RL agent look good.

The temporal split with seasonal and geographic zone stratification addresses two genuine deployment concerns simultaneously: temporal leakage (the agent cannot learn future traffic patterns) and distributional shift from seasonal/geographic variation. Stratifying within each temporal block by season and urban zone (downtown, residential, industrial) ensures that the test set is not accidentally dominated by a particular geography or season that happens to favor one method. The 12-18 month data collection window captures multiple seasonal cycles, which is the minimum required to evaluate seasonal generalization claims.

The DQN architecture is appropriately specified for the action space (10-20 candidate routes generated by a fast heuristic route generator) rather than attempting to learn routing from a raw GPS state space. This hybrid design — using a heuristic to generate candidate routes, then using RL to select among them — is computationally tractable and deployment-realistic. The composite reward (delivery time + customer satisfaction penalty) is correctly designed to prevent time optimization at the cost of satisfaction degradation.

The primary metric — average delivery time with bootstrap confidence intervals — directly answers the operational question, and the satisfaction constraint as an explicit gating condition prevents the evaluation from declaring success when customer experience degrades. Stratified test results by season verify seasonal generalization explicitly.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on combined train+validation data — validation statistics leak into feature scaling, contaminating model selection
Customer satisfaction rating used in reward signal is a lagged outcome not available at routing decision time — learned policy non-deployable
DQN trained for only 50–100 episodes on high-dimensional routing problem — insufficient to learn stable policy, comparison against heuristic inconclusive
Replay buffer of 10,000 transitions is too small for the state space complexity described
No evaluation of whether the learned RL policy is actually interpretable or safe to deploy in production routing
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on combined train+validation data — validation statistics leak into feature scaling, contaminating model selection
Customer satisfaction rating used in reward signal is a lagged outcome not available at routing decision time — learned policy non-deployable
DQN trained for only 50–100 episodes on high-dimensional routing problem — insufficient to learn stable policy, comparison against heuristic inconclusive
Replay buffer of 10,000 transitions is too small for the state space complexity described
